title: A multi-stage backdoor detection (MSBD) Framework
abstract: Backdoor attacks represent a serious challenge to robust deployment of machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) models in safety- and mission-critical fields. In a backdoor attack, an adversary injects a hidden trigger so that the model behaves normally when the inputs are clean but consistently produces attacker-chosen outputs when the trigger is present. Existing defences generally work at a single stage in the ML lifecycle—on data, on the model, or at inference time—and are thus susceptible to adaptive attackers that intentionally evade their underlying assumptions. This paper proposes Multi-Stage Backdoor Detection (MSBD), which provides a defence-in-depth structure that combines multiple mechanisms in the training, post-training model inspection, and deployment-time monitoring. MSBD has four stages: influence-based screening of training samples (Stage A), optimisation-based trigger inversion (Stage B), neuron activation graph analysis for detection of suspicious subnetworks (Stage C), as well as calibrated runtime detection with integration of trigger signatures and perturbation-based consistency checks (Stage D). The platform is intended to function under realistic defender conditions with limited access to both data and models, and for offline validation and online monitoring. We evaluate MSBD on three benchmark datasets (MNIST, CIFAR-10, GTSRB) under a strong BadNets-style backdoor attack and compare it against five representative defences (STRIP, Neural Cleanse, Activation Clustering, Spectral Signatures, and Fine-Pruning). Across all datasets, we find an average F1-score of 99.0% for MSBD, which is consistently better than STRIP’s, with a practical runtime overhead, showing that multi-stage, cross-layer defences can significantly improve robustness over single-stage defences.
text:
Received 12 January 2026, accepted 25 January 2026, date of publication 28 January 2026, date of current version 5 February 2026.
Digital Object Identifier 10.1109/ACCESS.2026.3659007
A Multi-Stage Backdoor Detection
(MSBD) Framework
HOSSEIN ABROSHAN
, (Senior Member, IEEE), AND SYED WAQUAS HASHMI
School of Computing and Information Sciences, Anglia Ruskin University, CB1 1PT Cambridge, U.K.
Corresponding author: Hossein Abroshan (hossein.abroshan@aru.ac.uk)
This work was supported by APC funded by the Anglia Ruskin University.
ABSTRACT Backdoor attacks represent a serious challenge to robust deployment of machine learning (ML)
and deep learning (DL) models in safety- and mission-critical fields. In a backdoor attack, an adversary
injects a hidden trigger so that the model behaves normally when the inputs are clean but consistently
produces attacker-chosen outputs when the trigger is present. Existing defences generally work at a single
stage in the ML lifecycle—on data, on the model, or at inference time—and are thus susceptible to adaptive
attackers that intentionally evade their underlying assumptions. This paper proposes Multi-Stage Backdoor
Detection (MSBD), which provides a defence-in-depth structure that combines multiple mechanisms in
the training, post-training model inspection, and deployment-time monitoring. MSBD has four stages:
influence-based screening of training samples (Stage A), optimisation-based trigger inversion (Stage B),
neuron activation graph analysis for detection of suspicious subnetworks (Stage C), as well as calibrated
runtime detection with integration of trigger signatures and perturbation-based consistency checks (Stage D).
The platform is intended to function under realistic defender conditions with limited access to both data
and models, and for offline validation and online monitoring. We evaluate MSBD on three benchmark
datasets (MNIST, CIFAR-10, GTSRB) under a strong BadNets-style backdoor attack and compare it against
five representative defences (STRIP, Neural Cleanse, Activation Clustering, Spectral Signatures, and Fine-
Pruning). Across all datasets, we find an average F1-score of 99.0% for MSBD, which is consistently
better than STRIP’s, with a practical runtime overhead, showing that multi-stage, cross-layer defences can
significantly improve robustness over single-stage defences.
INDEX TERMS Backdoor attacks, trojan attacks, adversarial machine learning, runtime detection, model
inspection, critical infrastructure security.
I. INTRODUCTION
Machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) models
are increasingly being used in decision-critical systems on
critical infrastructure, including industrial control systems
(ICS), smart grids, intelligent transportation, and healthcare,
where high-fidelity decisions may mean the difference
between normal operation and catastrophic failure. However,
such integration exposes these systems to a growing spectrum
of adversarial threats with potentially severe safety and
economic consequences.
Adversarial manipulation of ML models is commonly
categorised into three broad dimensions: evasion attacks,
The associate editor coordinating the review of this manuscript and
approving it for publication was Tiago Cruz
.
which perturb inputs at inference time without modi-
fying the model itself [1], [2]; poisoning or backdoor
attacks, where the training data or training process is
manipulated so that the model learns a hidden trigger-
based behaviour [3]; and model extraction or compromise,
where an adversary attempts to infer model parameters
or internal logic via queries [4]. Among these, backdoor
poisoning attacks are particularly pernicious in safety-critical
applications. An attacker implants a hidden trigger so that
the model behaves normally on clean inputs but consistently
outputs attacker-specified predictions whenever the trigger
appears [5]. Because backdoored models typically maintain
high accuracy on standard validation sets, they can easily
evade conventional testing and propagate through ML supply
chains and third-party model distribution channels.
17874
 2026 The Authors. This work is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 License.
For more information, see https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/
VOLUME 14, 2026
H. Abroshan, S. W. Hashmi: Multi-Stage Backdoor Detection (MSBD) Framework
The stealthiness and specificity of backdoor attacks have
motivated a large body of defensive research. Data-centric
defences, such as Activation Clustering and Spectral Signa-
tures, attempt to identify poisoned samples via anomalies in
feature space [6], [7]. Model-centric defences, exemplified by
Neural Cleanse and Artificial Brain Stimulation, reverse engi-
neer potential triggers or identify suspicious neurons respon-
sible for malicious behaviour [8], [9]. Runtime defences, such
as STRIP and SentiNet, monitor inference-time behaviour to
flag inputs that appear to contain triggers [10], [11]. While
each category is effective in certain scenarios, most existing
defences are designed for a specific stage of the ML lifecycle
and often rely on strong assumptions about the trigger
(e.g., static, visible, or universal). As a result, individual
defences can be circumvented by adaptive attackers that
deliberately violate these assumptions, for example via clean-
label attacks [12], input-aware or dynamic triggers [13],
or distributed/backdoor representations.
To address these gaps, we propose Multi-Stage Backdoor
Detection (MSBD), a defence-in-depth framework that inte-
grates complementary detection mechanisms across multiple
stages of the ML lifecycle. MSBD combines influence-based
training data inspection, generator-based trigger inversion,
graph-based neuron activation analysis, and calibrated run-
time monitoring. This multi-pronged architecture raises the
difficulty for adversaries, as successful evasion would require
simultaneously defeating orthogonal detection mechanisms.
Moreover, MSBD is designed with operational constraints
of critical infrastructure in mind, enabling integration into
existing model development workflows and deployment with
lightweight runtime overhead.
This study investigates whether a defence-in-depth design
can provide stronger and more reliable backdoor detection
than single-stage defences under a standard but strong
patch-based backdoor threat model. Specifically, we address
the following research questions: RQ1) Does the pro-
posed Multi-Stage Backdoor Detection (MSBD) framework
outperform representative data-centric, model-centric, and
runtime backdoor defences across multiple benchmark
datasets? RQ2) How robust is MSBD to variations in
trigger location and intensity relative to the configuration
observed during training? Accordingly, our objectives are
to design an end-to-end multi-stage detection pipeline
spanning training-time screening, model-level inspection,
and runtime monitoring, and to empirically evaluate its effec-
tiveness and robustness against well-established backdoor
attacks.
The main contributions of this work are summarised as
follows:
• Proposes MSBD, a defence-in-depth framework that
integrates training-time screening, post-training model
inspection, and runtime monitoring for backdoor
detection.
• Introduces a multi-head trigger-inversion stage that
recovers compact trigger candidates to guide down-
stream analysis.
• Proposes
neuron
activation
graph
analysis
(with
modularity-based scoring) to localise suspicious Trojan
subnetworks.
• Evaluates MSBD on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and GTSRB
under strong BadNets-style patch backdoors, and com-
pares against five representative baselines, showing
consistent gains over STRIP.
• Reports ablation, false-positive impact (Stage A), and
per-stage timing results to support practical deployabil-
ity in safety-critical settings.
The rest of the paper is organised as follows. Section II
reviews ML security threats, backdoor attack models, and
existing defence strategies. Section III presents the MSBD
architecture and details each stage. Section IV reports
an extensive evaluation on three benchmark datasets and
compares MSBD to state-of-the-art defences. Section V
discusses implications, limitations, and deployment consid-
erations, particularly in the context of critical infrastructures.
Section VI concludes and outlines directions for future work.
II. BACKGROUND
A. MACHINE LEARNING SECURITY THREATS
Modern ML systems are vulnerable to a range of adversarial
threats that can be broadly categorised into evasion attacks,
poisoning attacks, and model tampering attacks [1]. Evasion
attacks (adversarial examples) perturb test-time inputs to
cause misclassification without modifying the model or train-
ing data [14]. Poisoning attacks modify the training data or
process to influence the learned model, potentially degrading
performance or embedding targeted behaviours [15]. Model
tampering or Trojan attacks directly alter model parameters
or training pipelines to implant concealed behaviours [1].
In security-critical domains, each threat class presents serious
risks to safe and trustworthy ML deployment [16].
B. BACKDOOR ATTACKS: THREAT MODEL AND
STEALTHINESS
Backdoor (Trojan) attacks are a particularly severe form
of training-time attack [17]. An adversary aims to inject a
hidden trigger or pattern into the model so that it behaves
normally on clean inputs, but reliably misclassifies inputs
containing the trigger to an attacker-chosen target class [18],
[19]. Triggers can be small image patches, stickers, or even
imperceptible perturbations. Because the backdoor is rarely
activated during standard validation, backdoored models
can exhibit near-normal accuracy on clean test data while
harbouring latent malicious behaviour [7].
Backdoor attacks are especially relevant in ML supply
chains, where data collection, training, and deployment may
involve untrusted third parties [8], [10]. Typical threat sce-
narios include poisoned third-party datasets, compromised
outsourced training services, trojaned pre-trained models
from model zoos, and post-training tampering on untrusted
deployment platforms [11], [20]. Early work such as BadNets
demonstrated that small patch triggers could cause targeted
VOLUME 14, 2026
17875
H. Abroshan, S. W. Hashmi: Multi-Stage Backdoor Detection (MSBD) Framework
misclassification in traffic sign recognition models without
degrading clean accuracy [5], [19]. Subsequent research
extended these attacks to clean-label settings, invisible or
blended triggers, and dynamic or input-aware triggers that
further challenge existing defences [12], [13], [21], [22].
C. BACKDOOR DEFENCE STRATEGIES
Backdoor defences can be grouped into three broad cate-
gories: data-driven, model-driven, and runtime defences.
Data-driven defences inspect training data or features,
seeking anomalies that indicate poisoned samples. Activation
Clustering clusters latent feature representations to separate
clean from poisoned samples [6], [21]. Spectral Signatures
uses covariance spectrum analysis to detect outliers in deep
feature space [7]. More recent methods such as SPECTRE
refine spectral approaches via robust statistics [23]. However,
clean-label, feature-consistent, and distributed-trigger attacks
significantly reduce the effectiveness of purely data-centric
strategies.
Model-driven defences operate directly on trained models.
Neural Cleanse searches for minimal perturbations that flip
inputs to each target label and flags outlier labels with
unusually small candidate triggers [8], [21]. TABOR and
ABS refine this line of work through additional regularisation
and neuron stimulation [9], [24]. Fine-Pruning prunes neu-
rons that rarely activate on clean inputs and then fine-tunes
the model to remove backdoor behaviours while preserving
clean accuracy [20]. Fine-Pruning is not a detection-oriented
defence, as it is not really a backdoor detector, but rather
a model repair method. Its aim is to reduce or eliminate
backdoor behaviour by modifying the model itself, usually
by pruning low-activation neurons and fine-tuning on clean
data, rather than identifying or warning on backdoored
inputs or models. Consequently, Fine-Pruning does not return
sample-level detection results like binary labels, confidence
scores, or precision–recall statistics. Rather, the performance
is indirectly established by decreases in attack success rate
(ASR) and changes in clean accuracy. In this paper, Fine-
Pruning is thus applied as a mitigation baseline, rather
than as a detection baseline, and is evaluated separately
from methods specifically for detecting backdoor presence.
Meta Neural Trojan Detector (MNTD) trains a binary
meta-classifier to distinguish clean from trojaned models
using probe queries [25]. These methods provide powerful
tools for model inspection and repair, but often assume static,
universal triggers and can be computationally expensive.
Runtime defences monitor inputs and model responses
at inference time. STRIP perturbs inputs and measures
prediction entropy, flagging inputs whose predictions remain
suspiciously consistent under perturbation as potentially
containing triggers [10]. SentiNet combines saliency maps
with patch transplant tests to determine whether a salient
region behaves like a backdoor trigger when applied to other
images [11]. Februus and related approaches perform input
preprocessing to remove or neutralise suspicious regions
before classification [26]. Runtime methods are attractive in
black-box or deployment settings but can be bypassed by
adaptive triggers designed to mimic benign entropy patterns
or distributed saliency [22].
A recent study introduced a post-training black-box
defence for all-to-one (A2O) backdoor attacks that detects
poisoned inputs by exploiting their higher robustness to
uniform noise compared to clean samples and training a
novelty detector on this ‘‘noise-resiliency’’ profile [27]. This
study’s results demonstrate strong performance across a wide
range of trigger patterns and locations in image classification
and A2O attacks in a purely post-training inference setting,
but the method is designed specifically for image classifiers
and A2O attacks and has no training-time nor model-
inspection techniques, which are central to the multi-stage
design of MSBD.
D. LIMITATIONS AND NEED FOR A MULTI-STAGE
APPROACH
No single category of defence is sufficient against the full
spectrum of backdoor attacks. Data-centric methods require
access to training data and often fail against clean-label
or statistically camouflaged poisons. Model-centric methods
may struggle with distributed or dynamic triggers and can
incur significant computational overhead. Runtime monitors
depend on stable trigger patterns and can be evaded by attacks
that manipulate entropy, saliency, or context. Attackers
have repeatedly adapted to isolated defences by designing
backdoors that specifically violate their assumptions [21],
[22], [25].
These limitations motivate integrated, multi-stage defences
that span the ML lifecycle. Recent surveys and empirical
studies increasingly call for defence-in-depth strategies
that jointly analyse data, model internals, and runtime
behaviour [21], [27], [28], [29]. By connecting evidence
across training, validation, and deployment, defenders can
raise the bar for attackers and reduce the likelihood that a
single blind spot compromises the system. MSBD is designed
explicitly with this goal: to orchestrate complementary
mechanisms at multiple stages and fuse their outputs into a
robust, calibrated backdoor detection signal.
Table 1 provides a conceptual comparison of represen-
tative backdoor defence methods across different stages of
the machine learning lifecycle, highlighting their scope,
deployment mode, and key limitations. This comparison
motivates the need for an integrated, multi-stage defence-in-
depth approach such as MSBD.
III. METHODS
This section describes the proposed Multi-Stage Backdoor
Detection (MSBD) framework. MSBD is designed to detect
a broad spectrum of backdoor attacks and to operate
across multiple phases of the ML lifecycle, covering
training-time data screening, post-training model inspection,
and inference-time monitoring.
17876
VOLUME 14, 2026
H. Abroshan, S. W. Hashmi: Multi-Stage Backdoor Detection (MSBD) Framework
TABLE 1. Conceptual comparison of representative backdoor defence methods across the ML lifecycle.
A. THREAT MODEL AND DEFENDER CAPABILITIES
1) ATTACKER CAPABILITIES
We assume the attacker can poison a subset of the training
data (dirty- or clean-label), or otherwise influence the
training pipeline (e.g., through outsourced training). The
attacker may use static patch triggers, blended or invisible
triggers, or dynamic/input-aware triggers. In this work,
we instantiate the threat model using standard BadNets-style
patch backdoors on image classification tasks, but the design
is intended to extend to more sophisticated attacks.
2) DEFENDER CAPABILITIES
During validation, the defender is assumed to have access
to the trained model, a small trusted clean validation set,
and limited metadata about the training process. During
deployment, the defender may only have grey-box or
black-box access, with the ability to query the model and
optionally apply lightweight perturbations to inputs. The
defender does not assume access to the full original training
dataset.
B. MSBD ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW
MSBD is not a loose aggregation of existing backdoor
defences applied sequentially. Instead, it is designed as a
tightly coupled, cross-stage system in which intermediate
outputs from one stage explicitly constrain and inform
subsequent stages. Training-time influence scores guide
trigger inversion initialisation; recovered triggers drive
neuron activation graph construction; and identified Trojan
subnetworks inform runtime monitoring and calibration.
This joint optimisation and evidence fusion fundamentally
differentiates MSBD from prior approaches that apply
individual defences independently and interpret their outputs
in isolation.
MSBD consists of four main stages:
• Stage A (In-training influence-based screening): flags
suspicious training samples based on gradient alignment
with a trusted validation gradient.
• Stage B (Trigger inversion): uses a multi-head gen-
erator to recover minimal triggers that cause targeted
misclassification.
• Stage C (Neuron activation graph analysis): con-
structs neuron co-activation graphs and scores suspected
‘‘Trojan subnetworks’’ using a modularity gap and
exclusivity metric.
• Stage D (Validation and runtime monitoring): combines
trigger signatures, perturbation-consistency checks, and
saliency patch transplants for sample-level detection.
These stages exchange information: Stage A influences
Stage B’s initialisation, Stage B’s recovered triggers guide
Stage C’s probing, and Stage C’s identified subnetworks
inform Stage D’s runtime monitoring and potential model
repair. A final score fusion module aggregates per-stage
evidence into a calibrated backdoor risk score.
C. STAGE A: IN-TRAINING INFLUENCE-BASED SCREENING
Stage A aims to proactively flag likely poisoned samples
during training. For each training sample xi, we compute
a per-sample gradient gi
= ∇θL(θ; xi) and a validation
gradient gval = ∇θL(θ; V) over a small trusted validation set
V. We then maintain an exponential moving average (EMA)
of the alignment score

and compute within-class z-scores over {Ii}. Samples whose
influence score repeatedly falls in the top-q% tail across
epochs are marked as suspects. To sharpen separation,
we inject small ‘‘confusion’’ minibatches drawn from a
trusted subset with randomly permuted labels and include a
confusion loss term weighted by α during training. Persistent
suspects can be quarantined and removed from the training
set; their removal and the resulting effect on validation
accuracy and attack success rate are used as additional
evidence of poisoning.
D. STAGE B: MULTI-TRIGGER OPTIMISATION-BASED
INVERSION
Stage B seeks to reverse engineer triggers that induce targeted
misclassification. We implement a lightweight multi-head
generator that, for each head k, outputs a mask mk
∈
[0, 1]H×W and pattern pk. Given a clean input x, the candidate

where ft is the logit for the target class and TV denotes total
variation. Diversity regularisation encourages different heads
to converge to distinct triggers. We early-stop when the attack
success rate on a validation batch exceeds a threshold while
the mask area remains below a minimality bound.
E. STAGE C: NEURON ACTIVATION GRAPH ANALYSIS
Stage C analyses internal neuron activations to identify
compact subnetworks associated with trigger behaviour. For a
chosen layer ℓ, we collect activations hℓ(x) over clean inputs,
trigger-stamped inputs using Stage B’s triggers, and random
perturbations. We then build a weighted graph G = (V, E)
whose nodes correspond to neurons (or channels), and whose
edges are weighted by trigger-conditional co-activation or
correlation. Community detection (e.g., Louvain [30]) yields
clusters of densely connected neurons. We implement the
Louvain community detection algorithm, greedily optimising
graph modularity by iteratively consolidating nodes into com-
munities to maximise the within-community edge density
relative to a random null model. Louvain is well-suited to our
setting, as it scales well to high-dimensional neuron graphs
and does not specify the number of communities in an a priori
setting. In MSBD, Louvain is used with trigger-conditional
co-activation graphs that encode neuron co-activation with
triggered inputs as edge weights. This helps in recognising
compact neuron communities whose activation patterns are
disproportionately influenced by backdoor triggers.
We compute, for each trigger head k, the modularity Q(k)
trig
of the graph under triggered inputs and Qclean under clean
inputs, and define the modularity gap

(4)
We also define a cluster exclusivity measure capturing the
fraction of activation attributable to triggers. The Stage C

which is calibrated on clean models to control the model-level

F. STAGE D: VALIDATION AND RUNTIME MONITORING
Stage D operates both in an offline validation mode
and in runtime. Offline, we optionally retrain a shadow
model on Stage A-filtered data and measure the prediction
disagreement rate (PDR) between the original and shadow
models, focusing on inputs with triggers from Stage B. Large
PDR concentrated on triggered inputs is further evidence of
a backdoor.
At runtime, we deploy a light-weight ensemble of
detectors:
• Signature scanning: direct matching for known triggers
(mk, pk).
• Perturbation-consistency (STRIP-style): apply ran-
dom overlays or mixes and measure prediction entropy;
low entropy across perturbations is suspicious.
• Saliency-based patch transplant (SentiNet-style):
transplant salient regions onto benign images and
monitor misclassification.
These signals, along with the Stage C score, are fused
via a calibrated logistic meta-classifier to produce a final
sample-level backdoor risk score.
Table 2 summarises the principal implementation param-
eters of MSBD, enabling reproducibility while avoiding
dataset-specific optimisation.
G. REPRODUCIBILITY NOTES
All experiments use the same MSBD implementation and
the same parameter settings across MNIST, CIFAR-10, and
GTSRB (Table 2). Backdoored models are trained with a
10% poison rate using a fixed patch trigger stamped in the
bottom-right corner, and are evaluated on clean and triggered
test inputs using the same protocol reported in Section IV-A.
For runtime monitoring, the STRIP-style component applies
10 random perturbations per input, and the fused detector
uses the same decision logic across datasets. Random seeds
are fixed for training and evaluation, and all metrics reported

To support reproducibility, the implementation and exper-
imental scripts used in this study are publicly available
online.1
IV. RESULTS
This section evaluates MSBD on three benchmark datasets
and compares its performance to five established backdoor
defence methods.
A. DATASETS AND ATTACK EFFECTIVENESS
We consider MNIST, CIFAR-10, and GTSRB, as summarised
in Table 3. All attacks use a 10% poison rate and a fixed
bottom-right corner patch as the backdoor trigger during
training. Table 4 reports clean accuracy and attack success
rate (ASR). In all cases, the backdoored models exhibit high
clean accuracy and near-perfect ASR, indicating successful
and stealthy backdoor implantation.
B. OVERALL DETECTION PERFORMANCE
We compare MSBD with STRIP, Neural Cleanse, Activation
Clustering, Spectral Signatures, and Fine-Pruning. Table 5
reports F1-scores across the three datasets. MSBD achieves
an average F1-score of 99.0%, outperforming STRIP by
5.9–9.5 percentage points per dataset. Neural Cleanse

ASR increases sharply as the trigger matches the training configuration,
confirming a strong, localized backdoor.

Table 6 focuses on MSBD versus STRIP, highlighting
MSBD’s consistent gains across datasets. We note that

is a model repair technique rather than a detection method

To assess whether the observed performance gains are
statistically meaningful, we performed paired significance
testing between MSBD and the strongest runtime baseline
(STRIP). Table 7 shows the test results.
C. ROC AND PRECISION–RECALL ANALYSIS
Figure 4 presents ROC and precision–recall curves for MSBD
and STRIP on the three datasets. MSBD consistently achieves
higher AUC values, demonstrating better trade-offs between
true and false positives.
Table 8 reports the ROC-AUC and precision–recall AUC
values for MSBD and STRIP across the three benchmark
datasets, confirming MSBD’s superior runtime detection
performance under varying operating thresholds.
D. MULTI-STAGE CONTRIBUTIONS AND NEURON GRAPHS
As Table 9 shows, removing Stage A leads to a small but
measurable reduction in detection performance, confirming
that Stage A provides a complementary auxiliary signal
that modestly improves downstream trigger inversion and
neuron-graph analysis rather than acting as a strong stan-
dalone detector.
TABLE 8. ROC-AUC and PR-AUC for runtime backdoor detection on three
benchmark datasets.
FIGURE 4. ROC and precision–recall curves for MSBD and STRIP on
MNIST, CIFAR-10, and GTSRB.
TABLE 9. Ablation study on the contribution of Stage A (training-time
influence screening) to overall MSBD performance on CIFAR-10.
TABLE 10. Stage A (training-time influence screening) false-positive
impact on MNIST. Stage A is evaluated as an auxiliary screening
mechanism rather than a standalone filter.
Table 10 quantifies the impact of Stage A on clean data for
MNIST and shows that, when used in isolation, influence-
based screening exhibits a high false-positive rate; in MSBD,
Stage A is therefore intentionally employed as a high-recall
auxiliary signal to guide subsequent trigger inversion and
neuron-graph analysis rather than as a hard data-removal
mechanism.
Figure 5 shows a typical neuron activation graph for a
backdoored model. Suspicious communities identified by
Stage C form compact, highly connected regions that are
strongly activated by trigger-stamped inputs but not by
clean inputs, providing interpretable evidence of a Trojan
subnetwork.

Highlighted communities correspond to neurons strongly associated with

Statistical tests (e.g., McNemar’s test and bootstrapped
confidence intervals) confirm that MSBD’s improvements
over STRIP and data-centric baselines are significant at
p
<
0.01 across all datasets (see Table 7). Confusion
matrices (Figure 6) illustrate that MSBD achieves high true
positive rates on triggered samples while maintaining low
false positive rates on clean inputs.
E. COMPUTATIONAL OVERHEAD
As shown in Table 11, MSBD introduces moderate overhead
during training due to Stage A but incurs only lightweight
costs during offline validation (Stages B and C) and low
per-sample latency at runtime (Stage D). Importantly, the
higher cost of Stage A is confined to training and validation
phases and does not affect deployment-time inference,
supporting the practicality of MSBD in resource-constrained
and safety-critical environments.
All timing measurements were obtained on a single work-
station equipped with an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4050 GPU
(6.4 GB VRAM), 24 GB RAM and a standard multi-core
CPU, using the same software environment across all stages;
the reported values are intended as indicative rather than
absolute and are used for relative comparison of MSBD
stages.
All experiments were conducted using a single, consistent
implementation of MSBD across datasets, with identical
detection logic and parameter settings. Random seeds were
fixed for training and evaluation, and all reported metrics
represent averages over the same experimental protocol.
The implementation details provided in this paper are
sufficient to reproduce the reported results, and the source
code and experimental scripts are publicly available at
https://github.com/habroshan/MSBD.
TABLE 11. Approximate computational cost of MSBD stages measured on
CIFAR-10. Stage A is reported as training-time overhead relative to
baseline training, Stages B and C are offline validation costs, and Stage D
is per-sample runtime latency.
V. DISCUSSION
Prior to the interpretation of the results, it is necessary
to make a statement on the extent of MSBD scope of
applicability. While our experimental investigation in this
paper examines the impact of strong BadNets-like patch-
based backdoor attacks on image classification models, it is
necessary to highlight the fact that the design of MSBD
is stage-agnostic and aims at different manifestations of
backdoor behaviour across the life of the model. Each stage
captures a distinct signal—training-time influence anoma-
lies, model-level trigger sensitivity, neuron-level activation
concentration, and inference-time behavioural consistency—
rather than relying on assumptions specific to visible patch
triggers. MSBD operates on this principle and adopts a
process-level design to capture multiple signals and distinct
latent inputs. Hence, while the presented results show the
effectiveness of MSBD under a well-known and practically
valid threat model, quantitative validation against clean-label,
dynamic, or domain-specific backdoors remains an important
direction for future work.
The empirical results confirm that MSBD represents a
robust and generalisable defence against patch-based back-
door attacks in heterogeneous image datasets. On MNIST,
CIFAR-10, and GTSRB, MSBD achieves very high detection
performance, with an average F1-score of 99.0% and
tangible improvements over the best runtime baseline, STRIP.
Simultaneously, the comparative analysis highlights key
nuances and limitations that characterise these gains and
point the way to future work.
First, the findings support that single-stage defences are
inflexible against modern, meticulously designed backdoors.
Based on both early work on poisoning detection and data-
centric methods, such as Activation Clustering and Spectral
Signatures [6], [7], [21], we achieved extremely low recall
and F1 scores across all three datasets in our setting. This
is consistent with emerging evidence that clean-label and
feature-consistent poisons can be made to sit comfortably
within the normal class distribution, efficiently neutralising
simple outlier-based detectors [21], [22]. So, the result is
similar, as demonstrated by STRIP, still one of the most
common runtime detectors, which is significantly worse
than MSBD on F1 and AUC, especially on CIFAR-10 and
GTSRB [10], where visual variability is higher, and triggers
interact with complex backgrounds. Our experiments thus
VOLUME 14, 2026
17881
H. Abroshan, S. W. Hashmi: Multi-Stage Backdoor Detection (MSBD) Framework
FIGURE 6. Confusion matrices for MSBD’s runtime detector on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and GTSRB.
provide empirical evidence for the prevailing view that
defences targeting a single phase of the pipeline or a single
domain of signals are less effective against adaptive, supply-
chain–style backdoor threats [21], [22], [25].
Second, it becomes apparent that we compare Neural
Cleanse [8], which makes MSBD complementary rather
than less useful than model-based inspection tools. At all
three datasets, Neural Cleanse performs perfect backdoor
detection, which is in line with previous work that implies
static patch triggers with full white-box access. But it has
a high cost per computation, and so is inherently built as
an offline vetting mechanism, not as a runtime monitor.
On the contrary, MSBD is meant to augment those tools,
not supplant them: Stage B uses the same optimisation
framework to recover triggers again, while Stages C and D
use those triggers for neuron-graph analysis and runtime
monitoring, respectively. MSBD is thus seen as an effort
to ‘‘operationalise’’ the findings of Neural Cleanse and its
counterparts within a larger end-to-end detection pipeline
(while still within the bounds of partial access and runtime).
Third, the analysis of neuron activation graphs in Stage C
provides insight into the internal architecture of the backdoors
and serves as a bridge from model focus to the run-
ning/operational perspective of the backdoors. The presence
of dense, close-knit neuron communities directly activated
in large numbers by trigger-stamped inputs validates earlier
speculation that backdoors are often driven by the localised
subnetworks [9], [21]. Unlike scalar anomaly scores, the
graph structure provides interpretable evidence that might
be useful in high-assurance contexts: security engineers
can, in theory, inspect or target suspicious subnetworks
for reparability and/or pruning or additional monitoring.
Meanwhile, the Stage C analysis reported here is validated
on a small number of architectures and attack families, and
the stability of the modularity-based score is still a question.
From a deployment perspective, MSBD is designed to
balance detection strength with operational practicality.
Stages A-C are executed offline during model development
or validation and therefore do not impact inference latency.
Stage D operates at runtime but relies on lightweight pertur-
bation and consistency checks that can be selectively enabled,
rate-limited, or applied to a subset of inputs, allowing
system operators to trade detection sensitivity for throughput
when necessary. As a result, MSBD can be integrated
into existing machine learning pipelines and monitoring
infrastructures without imposing prohibitive computational
overhead, making it suitable for safety- and mission-critical
environments where real-time constraints must be respected.
However, whilst these positive results are promising,
several drawbacks of the current study must be pointed
out. Lastly, the attack space explored in our experiments is
limited to high-ASR, BadNets-type patch backdoors with
a predefined trigger pattern and a distinct target class.
While triggers are probed at several points and intensities,
with MSBD’s runtime detector demonstrating a reasonable
resilience to such variations, the assessment has not yet
covered clean-label attacks, sample-specific or input-aware
triggers, or broader semantic triggers (e.g., warping-based
or style-based backdoors) that have been suggested in
previous work [21], [22]. The current observations should be
considered, therefore, as strong evidence of the efficacy of
MSBD in the presence of a well-characterised, although still
quite structured, class of backdoors, and not as a full picture
of its behaviour with every known attack type.
A second limitation relates to the relative weakness of
Stage A in its own instantiation. Influence-based screening
identifies a section of poisoned samples, but its precision
and recall in MNIST are low, and its major role in our
experiments is as a slight pre-filter and auxiliary signal for
the subsequent steps. This result is in line with the intrinsic
challenge of distinguishing feature-consistent poisons from
clean data on the basis of gradient-based heuristics alone.
It also highlights an aspect of the design philosophy of
MSBD: the framework does not assume that Stage A is
a strong detector alone, but exploits its partial information
and transforms that into a stronger trigger inversion and
neuron-graph model. However, the approach in the future
could be improved with more robust training-time screening
mechanisms, perhaps including a representation learning
strategy or robust statistics beyond just influence scores.
Third, although Stage D shows outstanding results in
our experiments, the possibility exists that its detectors
could overfit to the specific class of patch triggers used for
training and testing. The trade-off between the ‘‘Optimised’’,
17882
VOLUME 14, 2026
H. Abroshan, S. W. Hashmi: Multi-Stage Backdoor Detection (MSBD) Framework
‘‘Balanced’’, and ‘‘Generalised’’ detectors observed with
MNIST already suggests that it is difficult to design runtime
detectors that are highly accurate to known trigger families
while being very robust to unknown variants. Operators are
often expected to operate at a more conservative level of
operation in safety-critical deployments (in which loss of
recall occurs to minimise false positives) whenever manual
audit/testing of alarms is expensive. The inclusion of explicit
cost models and human-in-the-loop constraints in the design
and calibration of Stage D is thus an important pragmatic
factor that is not addressed in this work.
Finally, our evaluation focuses solely on image classifica-
tion benchmarks. While these datasets span different levels of
complexity and class cardinality, they do not capture the full
diversity of application domains in which backdoor threats
are most concerning, such as industrial control, autonomous
driving stacks, medical imaging, or object detection and
segmentation. The design of the MSBD was partly driven
by critical infrastructure and ICS/OT application scenarios,
but we have not confirmed it for time-series process data,
cyber-physical control loops or detection backdoors in object
detectors. Thus, under these conditions, the assumptions of
Stage B’s perturbation model, Stage C’s graph construction,
and Stage D’s perturbation strategies might not be robust.
Summing up, these limitations indicate several concrete
lines of future research. Future work would benefit from a
more robust test of robustness by systematically validating
MSBD against attacks such as clean-label, input-aware, and
sample-specific backdoors, and against adaptive attackers
that fine-tune triggers explicitly to minimise MSBD’s fused
detection score. On the model level, extending neuron-graph
analysis to transformer-based architectures and large-scale
vision/backbone models would also explore the scalability
of Stage C. On the domain level, porting MSBD to ICS/OT
benchmarks and object detection backdoor benchmarks
would be helpful to ascertain its applicability to the
safety-critical environments upon which it was designed.
On the systems side, linking MSBD to model governance
pipelines and incident response procedures, and measuring
its impact on operational risk and cost, would bring the
framework into closer contact with practical deployment in
real-world critical infrastructure contexts.
VI. CONCLUSION
In this paper, we introduced MSBD, a multi-stage framework
for detecting backdoor attacks on deep learning mod-
els. MSBD includes training-time influence-based sample
screening, generator-based trigger inversion, neuron activa-
tion graph analysis, and runtime monitoring and is capable of
working under realistic defender capabilities.
In strong backdoor attacks on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and
GTSRB, an extensive set of experiments shows MSBD
is among the state-of-the-art methods when it comes to
detection, with an average F1-score of 99.0% and consistent
improvements over STRIP, the strongest runtime baseline.
MSBD provides a robust and practical defence-in-depth for
backdoor detection in safety-critical applications by incor-
porating complementary signals across the ML lifecycle.
MSBD will be extended in future work towards adaptive and
dynamic backdoors, object detection models, and industrial
control and cyber-physical systems.
In general, MSBD exhibits promising and stable detec-
tion performance across MNIST, CIFAR-10, and GTSRB,
with a mean F1-score of 99.0%, substantially surpassing
performance over the strongest runtime baseline. Meanwhile,
the existing evaluation is restricted to patch-based backdoor
attacks on image classification models, and certain stages—
such as training-time influence screening—primarily serve as
auxiliary signals rather than standalone detectors. It is hoped
that the next efforts can generalise MSBD to other backdoor
families as well, for example, to clean-label and input-aware
attack families, and also to other fields like object detection
and industrial control systems, therefore extending it to real-
life deployments.

HOSSEIN ABROSHAN (Senior Member, IEEE)
is currently a Senior Lecturer in cybersecurity
and AI with Anglia Ruskin University (ARU),
Cambridge, U.K., where he is the Course Director
for the M.Sc. Cyber Security and the Director of
the Cyber Security, Networking and Applications
(CNA) Research Group. He held several industry
roles in cyber security and is actively involved
in professional services, including organizing and
chairing tracks at international cyber security
conferences. He has led and contributed to several U.K. and EU funded
projects. His research interests include AI for cyber security, with a particular
focus on protecting critical infrastructures and OT/ICS environments, the
security and privacy of AI and machine learning systems, and human factors
in cyber security and cyberpsychology.
SYED WAQUAS HASHMI is currently a Machine
Learning Engineer and Cybersecurity Specialist,
with experience in AI security, intrusion detection
systems, data engineering, and cloud technologies.
He has worked with tools, such as TensorFlow,
PyTorch, Spark, Hadoop, and Kafka, and has built
scalable pipelines across AWS, Azure, and GCP.
His research interests include adversarial machine
learning, real-time analytics, federated learning,
and secure data processing.
